title: Signature Coverage Gaps
dataSources: Sensor health reports, network segment inventory
spl:
| inputlookup network_segments.csv
| join type=left max=1 segment_name
    [search index=ids sourcetype="snort:alert" earliest=-7d
     | stats count by sensor, segment_name]
| where isnull(count) OR count=0
| table segment_name, expected_sensor, count
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable: this use case combines lookup inventory with raw `index=ids` counts. It is not a single CIM tstats over Intrusion_Detection. Fix lookups and time range first.
description: Identifying network segments without IDS coverage ensures comprehensive threat detection across the infrastructure.
value: Identifying network segments without IDS coverage ensures comprehensive threat detection across the infrastructure.
grandmaExplanation: We compare our network map to the alerts we are actually seeing so we are not blind on segments where a sensor is down, mis-aimed, or missing.
implementation: Maintain network segment inventory with expected IDS sensor mapping. Compare against actual alert data. Alert when a segment has no IDS events for >7 days (sensor may be down or misconfigured).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: IDS sensor health monitoring.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Sensor health reports, network segment inventory.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Maintain network segment inventory with expected IDS sensor mapping. Compare against actual alert data. Alert when a segment has no IDS events for >7 days (sensor may be down or misconfigured).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| inputlookup network_segments.csv
| join type=left max=1 segment_name
    [search index=ids sourcetype="snort:alert" earliest=-7d
     | stats count by sensor, segment_name]
| where isnull(count) OR count=0
| table segment_name, expected_sensor, count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Signature Coverage Gaps** — Identifying network segments without IDS coverage ensures comprehensive threat detection across the infrastructure.

Documented **Data sources**: Sensor health reports, network segment inventory. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): IDS sensor health monitoring. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(count) OR count=0` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Signature Coverage Gaps**): table segment_name, expected_sensor, count


Step 3 — Validate
Compare the lookup’s expected coverage with your real network design and with each sensor’s management view (Snort, Suricata, or Firepower). Confirm whether a silent segment is truly idle, newly built, or missing a feed—then verify Splunk inputs, time zones, and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (uncovered segments), Status grid (segment × coverage), Single value (coverage %).
visualization: Table (uncovered segments), Status grid (segment × coverage), Single value (coverage %).
knownFalsePositives: Legitimately quiet segments (cold disaster recovery, unused labs) may have no traffic and therefore no alerts even when sensors are fine. Stale network inventories after redesigns can falsely show a gap; refresh segment and sensor mappings before you treat silence as a failure.
